injections.notes: Clean truth table construction and correct identification of the falsifying condition. Noted vacuous truth unprompted — shows genuine understanding beyond recall. No friction here.

Q3: Recognised the need to negate a universally quantified conditional but applied De Morgan's law for conjunction (not-A or not-B) instead of negating an implication (A and not-B). The set-theoretic framing displaced the quantifier logic. Q7: Flipped quantifier order, losing the fixed-witness structure of the existential. Attempted to formalise as a conditional between domain memberships rather than as a nested quantifier. Knows the vocabulary but the schema for negating implications and nested quantifiers is not stable.

Q5: Correct intuition and a gesture toward contradiction, but no axioms named or invoked. Student flagged not knowing what field/order axioms are — the gap is definitional, not logical. The real number order structure (positive cone, closure under multiplication) has not been encountered formally.

Q2: Correct interval intuition, correct exclusion of 0, but set-builder syntax was informal — wrote an interval label inside braces rather than a predicate. Q4: Union, intersection, and complement were all correct. Cartesian product was confused with elementwise multiplication — the ordered-pair definition of A×B is not yet in place.

Q6: Correctly evaluated the limit and matched it to f(1), showing the sequential/limit intuition for continuity is present. Explicitly flagged not knowing ε-δ. Q8–Q10 passed entirely. EVT, compactness, and topological continuity characterisation are untested and likely unencountered.

Q1: Correctly stated contrapositive and truth-value equivalence without hesitation. Clean logical instinct. Q8 was a full pass — EVT and interval notation f:[a,b] were unknown, so the logical structure question couldn't be engaged with at all.

Q4: Correctly identified the universal quantifier structure and produced the right English negation, but negation of implication formalised as (P→¬Q) rather than (P∧¬Q) — a precise and recurring error. Q6: Attempted to formalise density of ℚ but collapsed two bound variables into one, losing the relational structure entirely. Q9a: Negation of the continuity statement was flawless — all three quantifiers flipped and inequality reversed correctly. Suggests the error in Q4 may be about domain translation rather than quantifier mechanics per se.

Q2: Correct inequality work and correct bounds, but interval notation not written in standard form — wrote -2<x<8 rather than (-2,8). Q6: Informally described density of ℚ but could not name it or connect it to Archimedean property. Q9c: Recognised that absolute value relates to stripping sign, but could not connect this to trichotomy or order axioms — the field/order language is not yet internalised.

Q3: Cartesian product elements were correctly identified but written as concatenated integers rather than ordered pairs — notation gap, not conceptual. Q7: Correctly analysed the open/closed character of each component axis separately but drew the wrong global conclusion (said clopen rather than neither). Could not apply the limit-point or open-ball definition to the product set. The intuition about boundaries was present but lacked the toolkit to formalise it.

Q5: Strong ε-δ proof — factored correctly, bounded |x+2| by restricting δ≤1, and landed on min(1,ε/5). Minor notational slip (wrote 2 instead of 1 in the min, likely a scratch). Q7: Reached wrong open/closed conclusion for the product set and explicitly flagged not knowing how to use the definition. Q8, Q10: Full passes — EVT, compactness, preimage, and sup are all unknown vocabulary. Q9b: Good intuitive description of discontinuity via the negated definition — understands what the statement means even without the theorem name.

Correctly identified logical conjunction for intersection but miscalculated the interval. Showed confusion about feasible region definition and interior point characterization. Needs reinforcement on set-builder notation interpretation and Cartesian product geometry.

Correctly identified p ∧ q for intersection. Understood contrapositive preserves truth value but confused inverse with contrapositive. Showed intuitive grasp of material implication but lacked formal precision in logical transformations.

Significant difficulty with quantifier negation. Failed to apply De Morgan's laws correctly for nested quantifiers and did not properly negate implication (should yield conjunction). Attempted Question 4 formalization but quantifier ordering was imprecise. Needs systematic practice with quantifier scope and negation rules.

Demonstrated uncertainty about completeness property despite correct intuition about boundedness. Asked 'What's the Archimedean property?' indicating gap in foundational knowledge. Correctly recalled compact = closed + bounded but couldn't connect to completeness. Needs explicit instruction on ℝ axioms and their applications.

Knew Heine-Borel characterization but asked 'What is a pre-image?' showing gap in topological continuity definition. Did not attempt EVT applications (Q8-10). Correctly identified boundary vs interior in Q3 but lacked rigor. Partial understanding of compactness preservation but unable to apply to specific functions.

Correctly applied De Morgan's laws to negate the quantified statement and identified a valid counterexample (n=4). However, failed to consider negative integers when defining the solution set for n^2 >= 10, listing only positive integers.

Struggled with set-builder notation over Z. Correctly identified the condition n > 3 implies n^2 >= 10 for positives, but missed the symmetric negative solutions (n <= -4) for the set {n in Z | not(n^2 < 10)}.
Strong procedural fluency. Correctly computed sequence terms and identified limit behavior. Distinguished correctly between attained infimum (n=1) and non-attained supremum (limit 1).

Correctly derived formal negation syntax ($\exists x \forall y (P \land \neg Q)$). However, truth evaluation was contradictory (claimed both original and negation true). Failed to utilize vacuous truth strategy (choosing $y > x$ makes antecedent false). Shows fragility in semantic evaluation of nested quantifiers, specifically the scope of \forall y.

Strong geometric intuition; correctly identified D as a closed disk and attempted a topological proof of closedness using complements. However, failed to explicitly invoke Heine-Borel theorem or connect compactness to the Extreme Value Theorem. Skipped the preimage calculation $f^{-1}((1/4, \infty))$ entirely.

Skipped the axiomatic proof for reverse triangle inequality variant. Solved quadratic inequality (n-3)(n-2) \leq 0 but missed the lower bound constraint (n \geq 2), resulting in incorrect set n \leq 3. Indicates a gap in handling compound inequalities over discrete domains.

Set-builder notation for the solution set was incomplete (n \leq 3 instead of 2 \leq n \leq 3). Failed to address the 'symmetry' aspect of the question. Needs practice with precise set definition over \mathbb{Z}.
The student did not demonstrate any understanding of propositional logic concepts.
The student did not demonstrate any understanding of quantifier syntax, nested operators, and negation rules.
